Well, that's an unappealing choice. Right? I'm an idiot. I can't figure any of this out. It's just I can't read a label. I can't figure it out. I just don't know. Is Pepsi Cola good for you or is it not good for you? Ah, ah, ah, ah, ah, ah. Right? So they can choose that. It's not as unappealing as I'm a fat slob with no discipline, but it's still relatively unappealing. Most people don't like to admit they're complete idiots about anything. Or there can be a conspiracy. Well, that's a lot more appealing. There's a conspiracy that is keeping me fat. That's why a lot of people like to believe conspiracy. If they're sick, chronically sick, they almost have the same three choices. If they are chronically poor, they almost have the same three choices. Pretty much any fecklessness, any negative outcome, one of the choices of beliefs is conspiracy. It's a very appealing choice. It's also more interesting. See actual facts, actual information tend to be kind of dull. So what's more interesting to talk about? That AIDS was a conspiracy to eliminate the black race from the United States and it was perpetrated by the government. How did AIDS come to be? How did it come to migrate to the United States? Which is more interesting. Well, the conspiracy is a lot more interesting. So there's an entire industry, if you are not aware of it, just of conspiracy stuff. He's kind of part of it. He's a relatively benign participant. But still, there's an entire industry of this. Whole catalogs of books and DVDs and speakers and they all tend to overlap, by the way. So people that tend to believe in one kind of conspiracy tend to believe in multiple kinds of conspiracies. And it finds its way even into popular fiction. So like the Da Vinci Code. Why did that succeed in fiction? It succeeded because there was already a bunch of people who believed in the conspiracy that it now dramatized. A lot of people thought they were watching nonfiction. They don't even make a difference, right? And so here's another one. Here's a current one that's being driven. So it's a current control. It's for financial newsletter. So here's what this requires you to believe. Think about this. It requires you to believe that there's a secret society. Okay? Well, there you go. Okay? Okay? I am deeply suspicious of this because I've never met anybody who can keep a secret. So my own belief system says, if I'd like to meet them, because I've yet to find anybody, anytime I tell somebody to keep this amongst ourselves, it never stays amongst ourselves. Ever. I've never met anybody. But you've got to believe there's a secret society. You next have to believe that they have controlled the world's wealth for 60 years. So now you've not only got to believe there's a secret society, but that it is made up of remarkably competent people. How many remarkably competent people, other than while you've been here, have you met? When's the last time you ran into somebody that you went, holy shit, this person's got their act together? I want to hire them on the spot and hand them the reins. What? What's twice a year, maybe? What do you walk away from most people thinking? Be honest. Holy shit, I can't figure out how this person feeds himself. I mean, I don't get it, right? But you have to believe they've controlled the, not just the nations, the world's wealth, all of it. Russian, Chinese, Polish, Argentinian, Martian, they've controlled it all and they've kept an iron grip on it with nothing going awry for 60 years. That's the second thing you've got to believe. So now, how many people have you met who are not only, they strike you as incredibly competent, but they strike you as somebody who could keep their act together uninterrupted at a high level of competence for 60 years. From Tuesday to Thursday, the Golden State Warriors can't make foul shots. Two days, they're hitting the front of the rim. The front of the rim. I'll miss, but I won't hit the front of the rim. Two days, 60 years, my ass. Then we've got to look at who they are. We've got to believe this secret society that is so secret, nobody knows about it. Here's a list of who they are. Here they are. It's a secret. Prime Minister of Great Britain, the chairman of Barclays, major executives from Amazon, the director of the World Bank Group. Wait a minute. It all looks so secret to me. Page one, you actually have to believe something that is clearly in conflict with itself in order to even go to page two. It's remarkable, isn't it? It is remarkable. So anytime people say to me, well, that's not really logical. Please. It doesn't matter. Because see people that this hits, of course, because this is selective, want to believe this to such an extent that they accept these facts as reinforcing of it rather than in conflict with it. See, this list reinforces the idea that these are highly powerful people who have controlled the wealth. It reinforces that. It completely conflicts with this. If this guy, Stephen Loeb, who's hawking the newsletter, knows who they are, I imagine a lot of people know who the hell they are. But because I want to believe the whole premise, I take these facts, I use them to reinforce the thing that it needs to reinforce for all this to be true. And I do not let it harm the rest of it that I also want to believe. That's how we do things. Pretty much all of us. Pretty much all of us. That's how we do things. So always given druthers, I am microing, not macroing. Okay? Win trillions, I've had to macro. They want to use one thing across 15 countries. The only thing we did is a Spanish language version. But all of the, like, heavy handedness about the entire economic system is rigged against you and you'll never get ahead through work. You need one big cash windfall to get you. So the whole story about why you should be buying lottery tickets, I've had to do a universal conspiracy against you, it's in their copy, of keeping you poor. And you are never going to break through that conspiracy by just working more hours or getting a better job or saving whatever. You need a big windfall to trigger your leap. And that's why you got to buy a lot of lottery tickets. I've had to lay it down generically. They haven't given me the choice. Now given the choice, you would talk to Asians about this very differently than you would talk to Brits. Because their pre-existing belief system is all different. It's all numbers and signs and all of that. The only symbol the Brits care about is the queen. So you got to do keep calm and buy lottery tickets. But you know, when you're doing it generically, see you can't do that. And so when you have a choice, you always micro. But some businesses can't or won't. Like a proactive. There's empirical evidence that if we talk to single and divorced parents, single parent households, we talk to them differently than we do to married parents. We get better results. Their guilt is much higher. It's much easier to poke them with guilt. But they're running a $500 million business. They make money throwing mud against the wall.
